ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Burmese pork vermicelli soup; closest_parent 'pork soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pork meat soup) is the closest available parent category; no specific Burmese vermicelli pork soup entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese pork vermicelli soup as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Burmese pork vermicelli soup weighing approximately 450 g, comprising rice vermicelli noodles, braised pork pieces, and pork broth with aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Pork noodle soup, estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: rice vermicelli (USDA SR Legacy #20065), pork braised (USDA SR Legacy #10006), and pork broth (USDA SR Legacy #06215), combined in representative proportions (35% vermicelli cooked, 25% pork cooked, 40% broth).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese pork vermicelli soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data using representative proportions: cooked rice vermicelli 35% (157.5 g per 450 g serving), braised pork 25% (112.5 g per 450 g serving), pork broth 40% (180 g per 450 g serving). Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Rice vermicelli GI reported as 53 (low). Pork and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load; overall dish GI estimated at 53 based on rice vermicelli as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese pork vermicelli soup. GI of 53 assigned based on rice vermicelli as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source, per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Pork and broth contribute protein and fat but negligible glycemic carbohydrates. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (53 × 45.9) / 100 ≈ 24.3, rounded to 24.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork broth, fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 1,710 mg sodium, representing 74–114% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1,500–2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl provides approximately 46 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic index of 53 (low), yielding a moderate glycemic load of approximately 24; portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and elevated sodium from broth and condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to saturated fat from braised pork (approximately 3.6 g per serving) and high sodium content; choosing leaner pork cuts and requesting low-sodium broth can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vermicelli Soup with Pork (ဝက်ကြေးအိုး အရည်, Wet Kyay Ar Oh Ye) is a traditional Burmese noodle soup dish consisting of thin rice vermicelli noodles served in a clear or lightly seasoned pork broth, topped with braised or stewed pork pieces. The dish is a staple of Myanmar cuisine and is commonly served as a main meal at lunch or dinner. The broth is typically prepared by simmering pork bones and meat with aromatics including garlic, shallots, and ginger, then seasoned with fish sauce and soy sauce to achieve a savoury, umami-rich flavour. The rice vermicelli noodles are soft and delicate in texture, absorbing the flavour of the broth. Garnishes typically include fried garlic, spring onions, and fresh coriander. The dish is dairy-free and not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets due to the use of pork and fish sauce. Nutritionally, a standard bowl (approximately 450 g) provides around 369 kcal, 26 g protein, 11 g fat, and 46 g carbohydrate, with a significant sodium contribution of approximately 1,710 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its moderate glycemic load, and of high concern for hypertension due to its elevated sodium content from broth and condiments. It is served at Burmese restaurants in Singapore, including Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese pork vermicelli soup exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data using representative proportions for rice vermicelli, braised pork, and pork broth. GI is estimated from rice vermicelli GI data per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific Burmese dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future approved regional database entry if available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient reliability across the component sources used. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in the component sources (USDA SR Legacy pork braised and rice vermicelli entries report 0 or trace trans fat). 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' in the input is likely a field survey platform misclassification; this dish is a Burmese pork vermicelli soup and is not related to Singaporean Lor Mee. 8. The dish contains pork and fish sauce and is not halal or vegetarian. 9. Sodium content is high due to pork broth, fish sauce, and soy sauce; individuals with hypertension should exercise caution.
